title: Provincial Budgets
dataSource: Provincial Finance Departments (as reported by Dawn & Business Recorder)
methodologyNote: Budgeted estimates compiled from each province's budget speech / White Paper as reported by Dawn, Business Recorder and other reputable outlets. FY2026-27 figures for all four provinces are from June 2026 budget reporting; KP presented its FY2026-27 budget on 20 June 2026 (Rs2.17tr). FY2025-26 detail for KP and Balochistan beyond their NFC transfer share was not independently sourced and is marked accordingly. Commentary is editorial opinion, clearly labelled, grounded in the official figures shown.

provincialSurplus.note: Cash surpluses provinces actually delivered in the first nine months, per Ministry of Finance Fiscal Operations. The combined Rs1,636bn already EXCEEDED the full-year IMF target of Rs1,464bn — even Sindh, which budgeted a deficit, ran an in-year cash surplus because provinces under-spend their development budgets early in the year.

sources.label: ProPakistani — provincial surpluses (MoF data, 13 May 2026)

provinces.name: Punjab
provinces.source: Punjab Finance Department (as reported by Dawn 2008300)

highlights: No new provincial taxes — financed by federal transfers and higher own-source revenue.
Flagship projects: electric buses (Rs168bn), Nawaz Sharif Medical District (Rs169bn), cancer & children's hospitals, IT labs in colleges.

commentary.summary: Pakistan's biggest province posts a record-size, tax-free social-spending budget — politically shrewd, but heavily dependent on federal transfers and thin on quantified fiscal surplus.
good: Record social spend — education Rs750bn (+15%) and health Rs500bn (+10%) — with no new taxes on citizens.
Big-ticket human-development projects: electric-bus fleets, new cancer hospitals and medical districts, and IT labs in colleges.
bad: Punjab's own-source revenue is only ~Rs1.2tr against a Rs5.9tr budget; the rest leans on federal NFC transfers, so fiscal autonomy stays weak.
Development (ADP) of Rs752bn is only ~13% of outlay — current spending and salaries dominate the envelope.
ugly: 'No new taxes' plus a big spending hike makes the cash surplus the federation is banking on look fragile — and the province quantifies it only vaguely as a 'substantial share to the federation'.
Branded mega-projects risk crowding out routine service delivery and recurrent maintenance.

sources.label: Dawn 2008300 — Punjab FY2026-27 budget (17 Jun 2026)

highlights.label: Health (total)
highlights.note: incl. development Rs181bn (+131% YoY dev health)

highlights: No new taxes — the largest budget in Punjab's history, financed by record federal transfers and higher own-source revenue.

commentary.summary: Punjab's FY2025-26 budget was the largest in its history, with a record Rs1.24tr development programme and an ambitious Rs740bn surplus pledge that underpinned the national IMF consolidation — but that surplus was explicitly conditional on the FBR hitting revenue targets it ultimately missed.
good: Record development programme — ADP Rs1,240bn (+47%), the biggest provincial ADP in the country, with ~Rs494bn (40%) for the social sectors.
No new taxes, while own-source revenue was pushed to a Rs828bn target.
bad: Own revenue (~Rs828bn) covers only ~16% of a Rs5,335bn budget; the rest leans on Rs4.07tr of federal NFC transfers — fiscal autonomy stays weak.
The headline Rs740bn surplus was conditional on federal transfers arriving in full.
ugly: The Rs740bn surplus the federation banked on assumed the FBR would hit its target — but FBR fell ~Rs1.2tr short in FY2024-25 and again in FY2025-26, so the consolidation math was fragile from the outset.
A 47% ADP jump raises real questions about execution capacity and the quality, not just the size, of development spending.

sources.label: APP — Punjab presents Rs5,335bn FY2025-26 budget

provinces.name: Sindh
provinces.source: Sindh Finance Department White Paper (as reported by Business Recorder & Dawn)

highlights.label: Development (provincial ADP)
highlights.note: total dev programme Rs720.4bn incl. foreign assistance Rs256bn & federal PSDP Rs64bn

highlights: Salaries and pensions raised 7%; minimum wage increased to Rs43,000/month.
No new taxes; development programme cut ~26% on expected federal transfer shortfall.

commentary.summary: Sindh again budgets a deficit and trims its development programme — protecting salaries and pensions while defying, for a second straight year, the surplus the IMF expects provinces to post.
good: No new taxes; 7% pay and pension rise; minimum wage lifted to Rs43,000.
Rs100bn Karachi development package targeting the province's economic engine.
bad: Provincial ADP cut sharply (~Rs385bn vs Rs520bn) — development is again the shock-absorber when transfers look uncertain.
Most of the Rs3.56tr budget still rides on federal transfers Sindh does not control.
ugly: Budgeted a ~Rs36.9bn DEFICIT for the second year running, undercutting the national fiscal-consolidation target that requires provinces to run surpluses.
That repeated stance shifts the surplus burden onto Punjab and the smaller provinces.

sources.label: PkRevenue — Sindh Budget 2026-27: Rs3.56tr outlay, no new taxes
ARY News — Sindh budget 2026-27 details
UrduPoint — Sindh proposes over Rs720bn development
24News — Sindh plans Rs719.9bn development programme FY27

highlights: Salaries raised 10–12% and pensions +8%; own revenue grew ~16%; several nuisance taxes (entertainment, restaurant, professional) cut or abolished.

commentary.summary: Sindh protected its social sectors and pay bill but openly defied the national fiscal-consolidation plan by budgeting a deficit — a politically defiant, fiscally risky stance.
good: Protected the social sectors — education Rs523.7bn (+12.4%) and health Rs326.5bn (+8%) — and raised pay (10–12%) and pensions (+8%).
Grew own-source revenue ~16% while cutting or abolishing several nuisance taxes.
bad: Slashed its ADP ~20% to Rs520bn in anticipation of a federal transfer shortfall — development is once again the shock-absorber.
More than Rs2tr of the Rs3.45tr budget rides on NFC transfers Sindh does not control, leaving it exposed to federal collection misses.
ugly: Budgeted a Rs38.4bn DEFICIT — openly defying the IMF-driven requirement for provinces to post a combined ~Rs1.46tr surplus.
That single decision undermined the federal consolidation story and shifted the surplus burden onto Punjab and KP.

provinces.name: Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
provinces.source: KP Finance Department (as reported by Business Recorder, Samaa, Dunya & Profit)

highlights.label: Health
highlights.note: incl. Sehat Card Rs80bn + MTI hospitals Rs80bn

highlights: Salaries & pensions raised 7%; provincial minimum wage proposed at Rs45,000.

Tax relief rather than new taxes: Infrastructure Development Cess cut 2% → 0.75%; hotel bed tax 7% → 5%; property-tax exemption for homes up to 5 marla (~200,000 households); professional tax abolished for minimum-income earners; 30% discount on industrial tax arrears.
Deficit budget of Rs48bn, to be met from provincial savings with no new borrowing.

commentary.summary: Presented on 20 June 2026 by CM Sohail Afridi, KP's Rs2.17tr budget is a tax-relief, social-spending plan — but it breaks ranks by budgeting a Rs48bn deficit just as the IMF programme asks provinces to run surpluses, and KP's chronic under-spend means delivery, not allocation, remains the real test.
good: Heavy social and human-development tilt — education Rs468bn and health Rs334bn (including Rs80bn Sehat Card and Rs80bn for teaching hospitals) — with a 7% pay-and-pension rise.

Genuine tax relief for citizens and industry: lower Infrastructure Development Cess and hotel bed tax, a property-tax exemption for small (≤5-marla) homes, and a 30% discount on industrial tax arrears.
A sizeable Rs524bn development programme funded without new taxes or fresh borrowing.
bad: KP budgeted a Rs48bn deficit — at odds with the IMF requirement that provinces post cash surpluses to anchor the national fiscal target.
Own-source revenue remains tiny relative to the outlay, leaving the province heavily dependent on federal transfers and disputed net-hydel-profit flows.
Only Rs35bn was earmarked for the merged tribal districts — modest given their needs and the political weight KP places on them.
ugly: KP's resource side still hinges on long-disputed flows — net hydel profit arrears and withheld ex-FATA merger funds — that Islamabad has repeatedly delayed, making the financing optimistic.

The province utilised only ~Rs1,025bn of its budget in the first three quarters of FY2025-26; on that record, much of this record-size programme risks going unspent again — execution, not allocation, is the binding constraint.

sources.label: The News — KP CM presents Rs2.17tr FY27 budget with Rs48bn deficit (20 Jun 2026)
Chitral Times — Budget at a glance: KP Budget 2026-27 (sector allocations)
The Nation — KP CM presents Rs2.17tr budget FY2026-27 (20 Jun 2026)

highlights: Only ~Rs1,025bn of the budget was utilised in the first three quarters (Jul–Mar) — a recurring under-spend.
Own-source revenue and sector-level allocations were not independently sourced.

commentary.summary: KP's FY2025-26 budget totalled Rs2,119bn with a Rs547bn development programme, but only ~Rs1,025bn was spent in nine months — the familiar gap between what KP budgets and what it actually delivers.

sources.label: Dunya News — KP budget figures incl. FY2025-26 comparison

provinces.name: Balochistan
provinces.source: Balochistan Finance Department (as reported by Business Recorder, Samaa & Profit)

highlights.label: Development (ADP)
highlights.note: new Rs106bn + ongoing Rs100bn; plus Rs45bn federal grants & Rs40bn foreign assistance

highlights: Surplus budget; no new taxes; 7% salary & pension raise; 5,000 new jobs.

commentary.summary: Balochistan posts a rare surplus budget with a sizeable education allocation — fiscally cooperative with the IMF consolidation line, but its tiny Rs170bn own-revenue base leaves it almost wholly dependent on federal money.
good: Budgeted a Rs45.7bn SURPLUS, directly supporting the national consolidation target the larger provinces have wobbled on.
Education leads the budget — school education Rs127bn plus Rs31bn higher/technical — alongside Rs96bn for health, tax-free and with 5,000 new jobs.
bad: Own-source revenue is targeted at just ~Rs170bn against a Rs1,089bn budget — over 80% rides on federal transfers Balochistan does not control.
Development (Rs206bn) still depends partly on Rs45bn federal grants and Rs40bn foreign assistance that may not fully materialise.
ugly: Pakistan's poorest province has chronically under-spent and under-delivered on development; on past form, headline allocations translate poorly into actual schools, clinics and roads.
A paper surplus is easy when capacity constraints mean money goes unspent — fiscal 'discipline' here can mask a delivery failure.

sources.label: Business Recorder 40425989 — Balochistan unveils Rs1,089bn budget FY2026-27
Samaa TV — Balochistan presents Rs1.089tr surplus budget

highlights: Provincial budget detail (outlay, ADP, own revenue, surplus) NOT FOUND in accessible reputable sources.

commentary.summary: Balochistan's provincial budget could not be independently sourced from reputable open archives; only its confirmed FY2025-26 federal NFC transfer share (Rs743bn) is shown rather than an estimate.
